sentence: Subjective:
HPI: Jerry, a 45-year-old male, presented with an ankle injury sustained while playing basketball with his son.
He landed awkwardly and twisted his ankle, resulting in pain and swelling on the outside, which is visible and bruised.
He reports difficulty walking due to instability and pain, particularly on the lateral side of the ankle.
He has been having trouble with stability and walking, which is a concern for him since he recently started working out and wants to avoid disrupting his routine.
Past medical history: [NOT MENTIONED]
Review of systems: [NOT MENTIONED]
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: The ankle is swollen on the lateral side, with exquisite tenderness to palpation, particularly on the lateral and medial deltoid regions.
Skin is intact, with brisk capillary refill, and no malalignment.
The patient reports pain with weight-bearing.
Test Results: X-ray of the right ankle shows a displaced lateral malleolus fracture at the Weber C level, with no evidence of medial or posterior malleolar fractures.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a displaced lateral malleolar fracture, which is likely to require surgical intervention to achieve proper healing and stability.
Problem list: Lateral malleolar fracture; suspected instability; suspected pain.
Plan:
I prescribed meloxicam to manage pain and reduce swelling.
I advised the patient to ice the ankle and keep it elevated regularly, especially at work since he works from home.
I also recommended using crutches to avoid putting weight on the affected ankle.
I will schedule a follow-up appointment to reassess the patient's progress.
Follow-up: I plan to see the patient again in 3 months to assess the healing of the fracture and to plan for further treatment or rehabilitation.
